coredata.dc:title: Solvent-induced switching between static and dynamic fluorescence quenching of N, S Co-doped carbon dots in sensing of Crotonaldehyde: A detailed systematic study
coredata.dc:creator: Shaw, Manisha
Samanta, Dipanjan
Shaik, Md Abdus Salam
Bhattacharya, Angana
Basu, Rajarshi
Mondal, Imran
Pathak, Amita
coredata.dc:description:
Blue emissive N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) (quantum yield = 12%) have been synthesized via one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis of optimal mole ratios of tartaric acid and thioacetamide and they are devised as a novel fluorescent probe for assessing the sensitive and selective detection of Crotonaldehyde (
C
H 3
C H = C H C H O
, CT) in aqueous and ethanol solution. In the presence of CT, the fluorescence of NSCDs was observed to switch between static and dynamic quenching in the two solvents – water and ethanol, although both exhibited a positive deviation from the linearity in the Stern-Volmer (SV) plots. The positive deviation in the SV plots in the aqueous medium has been attributed to a combination of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, while the deviation in the ethanol medium followed the static quenching model. The LOD values in water and ethanol medium are estimated to be 50 μM and 30.8 μM in the CT concentration range of 0–6 mM and 0–8 mM respectively. The fluorescence quenching mechanism in the two solvents have been differentiated and validated through fluorescence lifetime measurement using time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, shifting in absorption maxima using UV–Vis spectroscopy and energy level calculations. The energy level calculations of NSCDs have been carried out using cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement and that of CT from density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NSCDs
Crotonaldehyde
Static-Dynamic quenching model
TCSPC
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of NSCDs by CT in aqueous solution
Possible quenching mechanism of NSCDs by CT in ethanol solution

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge IIT Kharagpur , India for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) XRD pattern of NSCDs; B) FTIR Spectrum of NSCDs; C) HRTEM image and the corresponding particle size distribution histogram (inset) of NSCDs; D) Raman spectrum of the NSCDs.
(A) Deconvoluted XPS of C 1s (A); O 1s (B); S 2p (C); and N 1s (D) of NSCDs.
(A) Absorption and (B) Emission behavior of NSCDs in 100% water and in 100% ethanol.

Excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectra of NSCDs (A) in aqueous and (B) in ethanol solution. (C) Representative bar graph showing the strongest emission wavelength of NSCDs in aqueous solution at λ ex = 340 nm and λ emi = 440 nm.

(A) Fluorescence quenching spectra of NSCDs by varying concentration of CT λ ex = 340 nm in aqueous solution; (B) Stern – Volmer plot for the NSCDs – CT system λ emi = 440 nm, inset: linear fitted SV plot at lower CT concentration; (C) Modified Stern – Volmer plot for the NSCDs – CT system; (D) Variation in the lifetime ratio with various concentration of CT in aqueous solution.

(A) Fluorescence quenching spectra of NSCDs by varying concentration of CT λ ex = 360 nm in ethanol solution; (B) Stern – Volmer plot for the NSCDs – CT system λ emi = 425 nm, inset: linear fitted SV plot at lower CT concentration; (C) Stern – Volmer plot for the NSCDs – CT system at higher concentration showing better non-linear fit.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime decays and (B) UV–Visible absorption spectra of NSCDs in presence of varying concentration of CT; (C)Spectral overlap of absorption spectrum of CT and PL emission spectrum of NSCDs (D) Benesi – Hildebrand plot of NSCDs on gradual addition of the quencher in aqueous solution.

Energy diagram depicting
E
H O M O
and
E
L U M O
of NSCDs and CT in water and EtOH solvent.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime decays and (B) UV–Visible absorption spectra of NSCDs in presence of varying concentration of CT in ethanol solvent; (C) Benesi – Hildebrand plot of NSCDs on gradual addition of the quencher in ethanol solvent.

head.ce:title: Solvent-induced switching between static and dynamic fluorescence quenching of N, S Co-doped carbon dots in sensing of Crotonaldehyde: A detailed systematic study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, W.B, 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, W.B, 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Blue emissive N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) (quantum yield = 12%) have been synthesized via one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis of optimal mole ratios of tartaric acid and thioacetamide and they are devised as a novel fluorescent probe for assessing the sensitive and selective detection of Crotonaldehyde (
C
H 3
C H = C H C H O
, CT) in aqueous and ethanol solution. In the presence of CT, the fluorescence of NSCDs was observed to switch between static and dynamic quenching in the two solvents – water and ethanol, although both exhibited a positive deviation from the linearity in the Stern-Volmer (SV) plots. The positive deviation in the SV plots in the aqueous medium has been attributed to a combination of static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, while the deviation in the ethanol medium followed the static quenching model. The LOD values in water and ethanol medium are estimated to be 50 μM and 30.8 μM in the CT concentration range of 0–6 mM and 0–8 mM respectively. The fluorescence quenching mechanism in the two solvents have been differentiated and validated through fluorescence lifetime measurement using time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, shifting in absorption maxima using UV–Vis spectroscopy and energy level calculations. The energy level calculations of NSCDs have been carried out using cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement and that of CT from density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Crotonaldehyde (CT), a small α, β-unsaturated carbonyl compound with a pungent, suffocating odor and a major constituent of tobacco smoke [1], has long been recognized as a potent eye, respiratory, and skin irritant [2] and its prolonged exposure is known to cause genotoxicity and mutagenicity in various cell systems [3]. Considering the carcinogenic nature of CT, several federal agencies such as (Occupational Safety and Health Administration) OSHA, (the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health) NIOSH, and (American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists) ACGIH have set their permissible odor and irritation thresholds in the atmosphere to 2 ppm (5.7 mg
m
− 3
); making it necessary to have a simple, sensitive and robust detection technique in place [4]. Fluorescence spectroscopy-based sensing, being time-efficient and having excellent sensitivity and selectivity, is a viable alternative for CT detection using appropriate fluorescence probes. Of recent, carbon quantum dots (CDs) with small (<10 nm) & narrow size distribution, possessing polyaromatic carbogenic cores with numerous (emissive) trap sites on the surface have gained significant attention as optical sensors/probes [5]. The use of CDs as fluorescence probes is particularly favored because of their selective and unique excitation wavelength (λ ex) dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties [6], good biocompatibility, high solubility & stable dispersions in water and in all common solvents. Further, fluorescent CDs, synthesized from various sources, have been reported as a probe for the selective and sensitive detection of metal ions [7], biomolecules [8], photosensitizers [9] etc. However, fluorescence-based sensing of CT using CDs is yet to be reported to date.

To understand the PL mechanism of CDs and their sensitivity towards the external environment, it is pertinent to use solvents with known physical characteristics such as - solvation power, viscosity, density, vapor pressure, refractive index, relative permittivity and freezing/boiling point [10]. This is because the interactions between CD's surface groups and the solvent molecules significantly impact the optical characteristics of CDs [11,12] due to the solvent effect. For instance, Mei et al. reported an amphipathic CD with solvent-specific alteration of its optical properties (i.e., solvatochromic sift) and used them as a sensing probe for detecting Cu2+ in various solvents [13]. Further, Lee et al. [14] and Wen et al. [15] also reported solvent-governed optical properties of multicolor emission in heteroatom doped CDs and their role in sensing applications. Although there has been substantial progress in the synthesis of CDs and their solvent-dependent PL studies, yet there is no such report on how different solvents can induce different quenching mechanisms in the fluorescent CD probes. This requires an insight into the band gap energies of either the fluorescent probe or the quencher or both in the different solvents.

To the best of our knowledge, we for the first time report the selective and sensitive detection of CT by using blue fluorescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) as the fluorescent probe in two different solvents (i.e., water and ethanol). In addition, we report a solvent-induced switching between static and dynamic fluorescence quenching of NSCDs in the presence of CT in two different solvents. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of NSCDs has been established through fluorescence life-time measurements, shifting in absorption maxima, and energy gap calculations of NSCDs and CT in the two solvents.

The powder X-ray diffraction pattern of NSCDs (Fig. 1 ), shows a broad diffraction peak centered at ∼23.36° as opposed to ∼26° for pure graphitic carbon. In addition, the interlayer spacing predicted for the d002 plane was observed to be 0.38 nm in contrast to 0.34 nm for pure graphitic carbon. It can be attributed to poor graphitic crystalline nature, hetero-atom doping and small average particle size [16].

The functional groups present on the surface of NSCDs have been identified using FTIR studies, as shown in Fig. 1B. The peak at 638 cm−1 denotes the out-of-plane bending of the N–H bond, whereas the broadband at 3198 cm−1 corresponds to the typical stretching vibrations of the O–H and N–H bonds. Sharp peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 770 cm−1 are attributed to in-plane bending of the C–O bonds and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The stretching vibrations of C–N or C–S bonds and the CS bond, respectively, are shown by two distinct peaks at 1110 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1, confirming the existence of N and S heteroatoms in the NSCD. Additionally, the stretching vibration of the C–N bond may be attributed to the strong peak at 1351 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of the CN and CC group to the weak peak at 1650 cm−1. FTIR investigations can thus infer the existence of COOH, OH, and NH2 functional groups on NSCDs, which may contribute to their high solubility in aqueous media [17]. The bright field TEM image of NSCDs depicted in Fig. 1C reveals that the particles are well dispersed, spherical in shape and range in size between 3 and 7 nm (Inset, Fig. 1C), with an average diameter of ∼4.7 nm. The HRTEM clearly reveals the poor graphitic nature of NSCDs. Fig. 1D shows the Raman spectrum of NSCDs that provides information regarding the crystallinity of carbon dots. The spectra showed two peaks at 1357 and 1562 cm−1, which were ascribed to the D band (disorder) and G band (graphitic), respectively. The G band supports the E2g phonon vibrations of sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice, whereas the D band is associated with the A1g breathing mode of vibrations of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the termination plane of graphite [18]. A somewhat disordered graphitic character of NSCDs is shown by the intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG), which is measured to be 0.82.

XPS study was used to investigate the oxidation states and surface composition of the synthesized NSCDs. The XPS elemental survey of NSCDs showed four peaks at ∼164 eV, 284.5 eV, 400 eV, and 531.2 eV, which corresponds to the binding energy of S (2p), C (1s), N (1s), and O (1s) respectively, confirming the presence of sulfur, carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in the sample, as shown in Fig. 2 . From the high-resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra, the relative atomic percentage of C, O, N and S are calculated to be 46.24, 29.1, 16.47 and 7.91 respectively. The C 1s core-level spectrum (Fig. 2A) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 283.9, 285.3, 286.1 and 287.6 eV, which correspond to CC/C–C, C–S, C–O/C–N (sp3) and CO/CN respectively. Similarly, the peaks at 530.6 and 531.7 eV in Fig. 2B may be assigned to C–OH/C–O–C and CO respectively. The deconvulated peaks for N 1s core level spectrum (Fig. 2D) at 398.6 eV, 399.2 eV, and 400.1 eV are due to the presence of pyridinic, amidic and pyrrolic N respectively [19], while peaks at 161.4, 162.9 and 164.5 eV in Fig. 2C can be respectively assigned to S2p3/2, S2p1/2 corresponding to the -C-S-C- bonds [20] and organic disulfides (-C-S-S-C-) [21]. Thus, findings from the XPS were in agreement with the FT-IR studies of NSCDs.

UV–Vis absorption Spectroscopy: To study the optical properties of NSCDs, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of NSCDs were recorded in both solvents. Fig. 3 illustrates the absorption spectrum of NSCDs in water and ethanol, revealing two broad and weak absorption bands in the 200–250 nm and 320–350 nm wavelength ranges, respectively. The
π →
π *
transition of conjugated CC bonds produced from the carbon core is attributed to the band at 200–250 nm, whereas the
n →
π *
transition of CO and CN bonds is attributed to the band at 320–350 nm [19,22]. On moving from ethanol to water, i.e., on increasing the solvent polarity, a red shift from 229 to 240 nm for π→π* transition was observed, while the n→π* transition showed a blue shift from 340 nm to 318 nm. In π→π* transition the anti-bonding molecular orbital (π*) is more polar due to separation of charges than the bonding molecular orbital (π). Thus in presence of more polar water solvent the π* stabilizes more that decreases the energy gap between π and π* in turn resulting into red shift of the absorption maxima. In case of n→π* transition, the ground state is polar. Thus, a highly polar solvent, such as water, will tend solvate the ground state (n) causing lowering of its energy due to solvation effect. Hence, absorption maxima corresponding to n→π* transition tends to get shifted to shorter wavelengths. Further, the non-bonded electrons (n) form hydrogen bond with more polar water solvent making the HOMO (n) more polar in comparison to the LUMO (π*), which cannot form hydrogen bond with water. Thus, in the presence of more polar solvent, such as water, HOMO (n), on being polar, tends to get more stabilizes (in order n>π*), resulting in an increase in the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO and therefore causes a blue shift in the corresponding absorption maxima. Interaction between the solvent molecules and NSCDs plays a significant role in affecting n-π* transitions which are usually referred to as the major fluorescent centers for the bright luminescence in the CDs. Thus the excitation wavelength of NSCDs for water and ethanol solvent have been chosen around 340 nm and 360 nm respectively.

Excitation-dependent emission behavior of NSCDs: The excitation wavelength (λ ex) dependent emission behavior of NSCDs in aqueous and ethanol solutions have been demonstrated in Fig. 4 A and B respectively, at a fixed concentration of 0.01 mg/ml. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 to 320 nm, the emission wavelength of NSCDs (in an aqueous solution) remains practically unchanged at 409–420 nm. But upon increasing the λ ex from 330 to 410 nm, the emission wavelength maxima underwent a significant red shift from 440 to 487 nm with a steady decrease in fluorescence intensity. While the excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior of NSCDs when recorded in an ethanol solution, the emission wavelength maxima remains unchanged with an observable increase in fluorescence intensity on shifting the excitation wavelength from 300 to 390 nm. On further increasing the excitation wavelength from 400 to 420 nm the emission maxima wavelength changes from 430 to 451 nm. The multicolored luminescent behavior of NSCDs in aqueous and ethanol solutions may be attributed to the presence of CO/C–S/CN/C–N surface attached functionalities that can redistribute the charge on the surface of the NSCDs, resulting in decreased HOMO − LUMO energy gap which in turn results emission at a longer wavelength. In addition, Oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on NSCDs surfaces exerted different degrees of surface oxidation that can also lead to excitation-dependent fluorescence [23].

Considering the carcinogenic nature of CT, researchers have carried out their detection using various time consuming techniques and the results suffered from weak accuracy or low selectivity. Thus we have employed a simple and rapid fluorescence based detection method for CT in aqueous and ethanol solvents. Fig. 5 A shows the emission spectra of NSCDs in the aqueous solution before and after the addition of CT at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. After the successive addition of the quencher (CT), the fluorescence maxima of the fluorophore (NSCDs) observed at 440 nm quenched systematically. During the quenching process, no spectral shift in the emission maxima of NSCDs was observed. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of NSCDs was analysed and finely fitted with the well-known Stern–Volmer (SV) Equation.
(1)
F ₀
F
= 1 +
k
s v
[ Q ]
= 1 + k
_
( q )
τ ₀
[ Q ]
where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs in the presence of and in the absence of the quencher, respectively;
k
s v
is the dynamic quenching constant; k
q is the quenching rate constant,
k
q =
k
s v
/τ₀; where, τ₀ is the average lifetime of the fluorophore without the quencher, [Q] is the concentration of quencher (0–12.79 mM). The calibration curve is shown in Fig. 5B, where we achieved an upward curvature with a good polynomial fit between F0/F and the concentrations of CT with a higher coefficient of determination R2 = 0.996. At low CT concentrations until 0.006 M, a good linear fit was obtained with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.99 and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 50 μM by using the 3σ criterion, where σ is the standard deviation of the graph. The LOD result shows that the NSCDs-based fluorescence quenching technique investigates a better sensing strategy for the detection of CT through easier and faster fluorescence spectroscopy.

Quenching studies of NSCDs by CT in EtOH solvent were also carried out to determine the relative quenching efficacy of water and EtOH solvents. The fluorescence emission maxima of NSCDs (at 425 nm) were plotted against increasing quencher (CT) concentrations to evaluate CT's quenching efficacy for NSCDs and study the mechanism of fluorescence quenching in EtOH. The emission maxima of NSCDs showed a red shift from 425 nm to 440 nm on increasing the solvent polarity from ethanol to water. This shift could be ascribed to the presence of –NH2 functional groups on the NSCDs surface, which facilitate efficient redistribution of charge in a highly polar medium resulting a decrease in the HOMO − LUMO energy gap thus, leading to the emission at longer wavelength in water medium. Furthermore, oxygen-containing functional groups such as – hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the NSCDs surfaces may initiate surface oxidation leading to excitation-dependent red shifted emission in the fluorescence spectra [24]. Fig. 6 A demonstrates the decrease in the fluorescence emission maxima of NSCDs at λ ex = 360 nm on increasing the concentration of CT in the EtOH solvent gradually. Fig. 6B shows the corresponding SV plot. Except at very high concentrations, the SV plot exhibits linear behavior (inset Fig. 6B). The combined region was fitted with F0/F = 1 + α2x + β2exp (γ2x), where β2 = 0.94324 ± 0.43778 [25] shown in Fig. 6B, separate fit for higher concentration region with only upward curvature displays better fit as shown in Fig. 6C. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by using the 3σ criterion (where ‘σ' is the standard deviation) from the linear fit of the SV plot at a lower concentration of CT (inset Fig. 6B), which is found to be 30.8 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of NSCDs by CT in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of a fluorescent probe can be reduced by various chemical interactions between the quencher molecule and the fluorophore, including electron or energy transfer, excited-state reaction, and ground-state complex formation [26]. Fluorescence quenching can take place in multiple ways, with dynamic and static quenching being the most common. Dynamic and static quenching can be distinguished by temperature dependent fluorescence quenching study, or preferably by lifetime measurements, or variation in UV–Vis absorption maxima [27]. In static quenching, the quencher attaches to the sensor in the ground state to create a non-fluorescent or less fluorescent complex than the fluorophore. At higher temperatures, the complex's stability declines, resulting in a reduction in the static quenching constant. In contrast, it is seen that the excited fluorophore and the quencher collide in a diffusion-controlled manner during dynamic quenching. Due to more diffusion-controlled collisions between fluorophores and quenchers at higher temperatures, the dynamic quenching constant will rise as the temperature rises. A straight line emerges in the SV plot when the quenching process is 100% static or 100% dynamic, but when they are mixed, a positive deviation from linearity in the SV plot is obtained. The positive deviation from linearity obtained in the SV plot shown in Fig. 5B indicates that the quenching is not solely collisional; thus static-dynamic quenching model is worth examining for the NSCDs quenching by CT in an aqueous solution.

When a fluorophore is quenched by collisions and by complex formation both, the Stern Volmer equation is represented by modified a SV equation, stated below:
(2)
F ₀
F
=
(
1 +
k 0
[ Q ]
)
(
1 +
k
s v
[ Q ]
)
(3)
F ₀
F
= 1 +
(
k 0
+
k
s v
)
[ Q ]
+
k 0
k
s v
[ Q ]
2
where
k 0
,
k
s v
represents static and dynamic (S-D) SV constants, F0/F are the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs in the presence and absence of the quencher [28]. We employed the modified SV equation to determine the S–D combination, which states that if the apparent quenching constant (Kapp) is plotted against the quencher concentration [Q], the result should be a straight line if the quenching is due to a S–D combination. The modified Stern–Volmer equation is given below.
(4)
K
a p p
=
(
F ₀ − F
F
)
/
[ Q ]
=
(
k 0
+
k
s v
)
+
k 0
k
s v
[ Q ]

Fig. 5C shows linear fitting between K
app Vs. concentration of the quencher, i.e., modified Stern Volmer plot, with a degree of correlation (R2) greater than 0.96. The values of
k
s v
and
k 0
were calculated by both eqn. (3) and eqn. (4) that cross-match nicely. The
k
s v
and
k 0
values obtained from the linear plot of the modified SV plot, through least-squares fitting using eqn. (4) were 79.8
M
− 1
and 3.41
M
− 1
. The
k
s v
values acquired from the initial linear range of Fig. 4C at low concentration is 82.8
M
− 1
. It can be seen from the calculated
k
s v
values that they are favourably comparable, which could satisfactorily explain the quenching curves to be combined static-dynamic quenching model. Further, the positive divergence from linearity observed in the SV plot of the NSCDs - CT system accounts for a static component in the quenching of NSCDs [29]. To elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence decay kinetics of NSCDs have been studied through time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) under different concentrations of CT. The fluorescence decay data could be fitted to a tri-exponential function, from which the lifetime of the NSCDs was estimated to be 4.14 ns. The average fluorescence lifetimes of NSCDs (4.14 ns) steadily decrease from 4.14 ns to 3.31 ns with increasing quencher concentration (CT), as seen in Fig. 7 A, implying the presence of a dynamic quenching mechanism. Deconvolution was used to fit the acquired fluorescence lifetime decay data. The average lifetime values in the absence and presence of varying CT concentrations were calculated to be 4.14, 4.13, 4.04, 3.84, 3.77, and 3.318 ns, respectively. These calculated lifetime data show a negligible decrease in the lifetime at lower CT (quencher) concentration, whereas a sudden decrease in lifetime was observed at higher quencher concentration, indicating that the non-linear SV plot (Fig. 5B) shows a static quenching process at lower CT Conc. (till 0.006 M) and a combined S-D quenching process at higher CT concentrations. Fig. 5D shows a plot of τ₀/τ vs. [Q], with the labels τ₀, τ and [Q] denoting their usual meanings as indicated before. The
k q
(the bimolecular quenching constant) obtained from the slope of τ₀/τ vs. [Q] graph is
1.78 ×
10 10
M
− 1
S
− 1
, further substantiating that the quenching process is dynamic in nature, controlled by diffusion of the donor and acceptor in the excited state. We studied the absorption behavior of NSCDs in the presence of CT at various concentrations to better understand the fluorescence quenching process. Fig. 7B demonstrates the shift in the absorption spectra of NSCDs as the concentration of CT increases. With increasing concentrations of CT from
7.8 ×
10
− 5
to
38.7 ×
10
− 5
M, the variation in the absorption peak position was observed to be monotonically red-shifted. A regular shift in the absorption peak position reveals the interaction between NSCDs and CT, which is particularly noteworthy. In static quenching, the formation of a ground state complex with the fluorophore is anticipated to alter the absorbance of the quencher [30]. As shown in Fig. 7B the absorption maxima changed with the addition of CT, at lower concentration, in NSCDs solution. This observation further substantiates the quenching mechanism to be static in nature at lower quencher concentrations.

To establish the stoichiometry for the interaction of NSCDs with CT, the Benesi–Hildebrand method has been used [31]. The corresponding Benesi–Hildebrand equation is given below.
(5)
1
(
F ₀ − F
)
=
1
(
F ₀ − F ´
)
+
1
(
F ₀ − F ´
)
K ₐ
[ Q ]
where,
K a
is the binding constant, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of free NSCDs, F' is the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs – CT complex, and F is the observed fluorescence intensity at its maximum. Fig. 7D shows the plot of {1/(F0 - F)} vs.1/[Q], the linearity in the plot (R2 = 0.999) reflects the formation of a 1:1 complex between NSCDs and CT and the complex is in equilibrium with their free counterparts [32]. The corresponding equilibrium constant is nothing but the association constant, which can be calculated from the intercept (I) and slope (S) values from the linear fitted data in Fig. 7D (Ka = I/S). The calculated association constant is 58.71 M-1. The spontaneity for the formation of 1:1 NSCD–CT complexes is measured by their negative standard free energy change, which is about −10 kJ mol−1 at room temperature (298 K).

In general, dynamic quenching is either due to an energy transfer (FRET) or an electron transfer phenomenon. Possibilities of FRET is ruled out because one prerequisite for a FRET quenching process is that the emission spectrum of the donor (fluorophore) should overlap with the absorption spectrum (acceptor) [33]. Fig. 7C demonstrates that the emission spectra of donor NSCDs and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor CT have no significant overlap, implying that an energy transfer process cannot explain the quenching of NSCDs by CT.

In this context, apart from FRET, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) is considered as one of the primary mechanisms involved in the sensing of CT where, the fluorescence intensity of the probes (i.e., NSCDs) gets diminished in presence of the electron deficient quencher (i.e., CT). PET between the fluorescent probe and the quencher may primarily occur through two approaches; in the first case, an electron from HOMO of the probe (i.e., NSCDs) is excited to its LUMO on the absorption of a photon. When the LUMO of a quencher (CT) is relatively lower in energy compared to that of the probe then the excited state electron from the probe can get transferred to the LUMO of a quencher (CT), which then eventually returns to the ground state through a non-radiative decay pathway, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the probe [34]. This is regarded as an oxidative pathway, in which the fluorescence probe, NSCDs, act as a donor while the quencher (CT) behaves as an acceptor. The second pathway suggests the electron transfer from HOMO to LUMO of fluorophore upon excitation, the one electron moves from the quencher's HOMO to the HOMO of the fluorescent probe. If the energy of the LUMO of the fluorophore is higher than the quencher's LUMO, electron transfer occurs through the first pathway (oxidative pathway) whereas, when the energy of the quencher's LUMO is higher than the LUMO of the fluorophore, the fluorescence quenching occurs through the second pathway.

In order to understand the origin of the high sensitivity and selectivity of the NSCDs towards CT, the molecular orbitals (energy levels) of the NSCDs (donor) have been determined through cyclic voltammetry studies [35]. The HOMO and LUMO of CT have been calculated using density functional theory (DFT) analysis at the B3LYP/6-31G* level and IEF-PCM solvent model in water and ethanol solvent, through GAUSSIAN 06 software [36]. The energy of HOMO (EHOMO) and LUMO (ELUMO) of NSCDs could be estimated according to the empirical formula:
(6) E band-gap = ELUMO - EHOMO … … … …
(7) E band-gap = hc ⁄ λ = 1240 ⁄ (λ) …. … … …

Where, E band-gap is the band-gap energy of NSCDs, derived from the absorption edge in the UV–Vis spectrum, and was calculated to be 2.4 eV. The anodic sweep of the voltammogram revealed no distinct oxidation peak, but the reverse sweep revealed a broad reduction peak at a potential, Ered, of −0.85 V when the reference electrode potential was +0.197 V for Ag/Ag+ (vs. NHE). The reduction potential of NSCDs, (Ered vs. NHE), that corresponds to the conduction band energy (CB) or ELUMO of the NSCDs, and was calculated as: Ered = (−0.85 + 0.197) V = – 0.63 V. The value of – 0.63 V (vs. NHE) in eV (vs. vacuum) is given by Ref. [37]: 4.5 eV (i.e., 0 V vs. NHE) – (– 0.63 V) = – 3.87 ≈ – 3.87 eV. The LUMO of NSCDs is therefore fixed at – 3.87 eV. Thus the E HOMO of NSCDs is calculated to be: (– 3.87–2.4) eV = – 6.27 eV (vs. vacuum).

Both DFT calculations and CV measurements revealed that
E
H O M O
and
E
L U M O
of NSCDs were in energy higher than those of CT, as shown in Fig. 8 , thus, favoring the electron transfer through the oxidative pathway. Thus, aldehydes are acknowledged as an electron acceptor and NSCDs act as an electron donor in this study. The high sensitivity and selectivity of NSCDs towards CT through static-dynamic quenching mechanism in water solvent compared to the static quenching in EtOH solvent can be attributed to the lower ELUMO value of the CT in water medium (−5.5 eV) compared to that in ethanol (−5.0 eV). This facilitated the electron transfer process between the donor (NSCDs) and the acceptor (CT) resulting in the dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of NSCDs by CT in ethanol solution
ce:section.ce:para:
Non-linear curvature obtained from SV plot through NSCDs quenching by CT in ethanol solvent can be caused by a variety of causes, including the combined effect of static and dynamic quenching, limited quencher accessibility, or a large amount of quenching. Dynamic quenching is not present in this situation due to no change in fluorescence lifetime was observed from the time resolved studies on addition of quencher, as seen in Fig. 9 A. When the extent of quenching is large, positive deviations from the Stern-Volmer equations have been observed. A "sphere of action" within which the likelihood of quenching equals unity is commonly used to describe such a situation.
F 0
/ F
=
(
1 +
K D
[ Q ]
)
e
[ Q ]
V n
1000
;
is the "modified variant of the Stern-Volmer equation." In this study,
F 0
/ F
=
(
1 +
K D
[ Q ]
)
e
[ Q ]
V n
1000
does not provide a decent fit, where V is the sphere's volume, representing the effective radius within which the chance of quenching is unity. When the contribution of dynamic quenching is small, KD ≈ 0, this equation changes to simply exponential growth. A shift in the UV–Vis absorption maxima implies a high likelihood of static quenching, which occurs when non-fluorescent complexes develop, as shown in Fig. 9B. After absorbing light, such a complex return to its ground state without emitting a photon. Fluorescence lifetime measurements corroborate this theory. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra for various concentrations of CT were recorded with continuous addition of CT (quencher), as shown in Fig. 9A. All of the spectra can be seen lying on top of one another. This rules out the idea of dynamic quenching, as such a process would reduce the fluorescence excited state lifetime [38]. Similarly, to establish the stoichiometry for the interaction of NSCDs with CT, the Benesi–Hildebrand method has been used [31]. Fig. 9C shows the plot of {1/(F0 - F)} vs.1/[Q], the linearity in the plot (R2 = 0.999) reflects the formation of a 1:1 complex between NSCDs and CT and the complex is in equilibrium with their free counterparts [32]. The association constant (Ka = I/S) was calculated from the intercept (I) and slope (S) values from the linear fitted data in Fig. 9C (Ka = I/S). The calculated association constant is 28.47 M-1. The spontaneity for the formation of 1:1 NSCD–CT complexes are measured by their negative standard free energy change, which is about −8.29 kJ mol−1 at room temperature (298 K). To understand the difference in the quenching behavior of NSCDs by CT in ethanol solvents, zeta potential (ξ) studies for NSCDs and CT in both aqueous and ethanol medium were performed separately. The ξ values obtained for NSCDs in aqueous and ethanol medium were found to be −33.8 mV and −28.2 mV respectively, that are significantly negative, indicating the presence of negatively charged surface groups –COOH and –OH on the surface. While the ξ values for CT in ethanol were observed to be less negative (−0.5 mV) than in the aqueous medium (−14.2 mV). Thus, substantial electrostatic interaction is expected between CT and the negatively charged NSCDs in ethanol medium than in aqueous medium due greater difference in their ξ values, which apparently facilitates the static quenching of the NSCDs by CT in ethanol medium [39]. The positive deviation from linearity in the Stern-Volmer plot and peak position shift found in the absorption spectra matches with the time-resolved fluorescence results, indicating that static quenching is the only cause of the quenching NSCDs by CT in ethanol solvent.

In summary, a simple and quick one-pot pyrolysis method was developed to fabricate photoluminescent N, S co-doped carbon dots using tartaric acid and thioacetamide as precursors. The NSCDs offered excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence property in aqueous and ethanol solution. The synthesized NSCDs was used as a unique fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of Crotonaldehyde (CT) through fluorescence quenching of NSCDs. The LOD value of CT has been found to be 50 μM in water solvent and 30.8 μM in ethanol solvent, which are comparable with the permissible limit reported in the literature. The fluorescence quenching response of NSCDs in presence of CT in two different solvents (water and ethanol) exhibited positive deviation from the linear Stern-Volmer (SV) plots. The positive deviation in the SV plots in the aqueous medium could be attributed to the combination of both static and dynamic quenching mechanism with the dominance of the dynamic quenching mode, while in an ethanol medium the deviation from linearity could solely be assigned to the static quenching model. In brief, on changing the solvent from water to ethanol, the fluorescence quenching mechanism changed from S-D to a purely static quenching model. Thus, NSCDs have been validated to provide a new, simple, low-cost, and convenient fluorescent-based platform for the selective and sensitive detection of Crotonaldehyde.
There are no conflicts to declare.

Manisha Shaw: Formal analysis. Dipanjan Samanta: Formal analysis. Md Abdus Salam Shaik: Formal analysis. Angana Bhattacharya: Writing – original draft. Rajarshi Basu: Writing – original draft. Imran Mondal: Writing – original draft. Amita Pathak: Corresponding author and checking/correcting the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge IIT Kharagpur, India for financial support.
